acceptance_targets: [ch12_dialogue_001] In the revised exchange at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:115 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:125, Tammy's answer to Lola's compliment no longer contains the phrase "good people get paid shit" or a trivially close variant, and it still reads as Tammy-specific bitterness about work, money, or Greg rather than generic wisdom. If the key reply remains recognizably the style-bible example, this finding fails.

[ch12_prose_001] By the end of the revised chapter, the performance-versus-real-decline dilemma is explicitly summarized only once across snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:19 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:149, and the closing lines at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:145 add a new observable consequence or decision tied to Tammy's scene instead of mainly rephrasing Lola's uncertainty about what kind of forgetting this is. If the ending still functions chiefly as a second abstract summary of the opening fear, this finding remains unresolved.

[PROSE_AUTOCOMM_CH12] PASS if: (a) all 11 cited instances are eliminated or replaced with non-formulaic alternatives rooted in Lola's specific sensory/narrative mode; (b) no more than 1 total instance of any single auto-commentary formula remains in the chapter; (c) at least 3 of the replacements use Lola-specific textures (embedded memories, Spanish, food/family imagery, story-form thinking) rather than generic analytical narration. FAIL if more than 2 instances of any formula survive, or if replacements use the same explanatory apparatus in different words.

findings.problem: Tammy's key reply, "Good people get paid shit," is a near-verbatim reuse of a style-bible calibration line. Because it lands inside the chapter's defining Lola/Tammy exchange, the beat reads imported rather than newly voiced, which damages voice integrity and violates the explicit do-not-copy rule for example dialogue.
findings.rewrite_direction: Replace Tammy's reply at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:123, then re-tune the surrounding exchange at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:115 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:125 so the turn still answers Lola's compliment without reproducing calibration text. Keep Tammy's high-contraction, flat, wage-conscious register, but give her a chapter-specific line rooted in her own pressures here (Greg, doubles, the apartment, the pay) rather than the copied phrasing.

findings.acceptance_test: By the end of the revised chapter, the performance-versus-real-decline dilemma is explicitly summarized only once across snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:19 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:149, and the closing lines at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:145 add a new observable consequence or decision tied to Tammy's scene instead of mainly rephrasing Lola's uncertainty about what kind of forgetting this is. findings.problem: The chapter states Lola's performance-versus-decline fear clearly in the opening movement, then states it again in almost the same conceptual terms at the close. Because the ending re-explains 'which kind of forgetting' after the courtyard scene has already embodied the cost of that uncertainty, the final beat lands as paraphrase instead of escalation.
findings.rewrite_direction: Keep one direct articulation of the central ambiguity in either snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:19 or snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:149. Rework the other cited span, plus the immediate close at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_12.md:145, so the chapter ends on new concrete fallout from Tammy's visit: a fresh sensory consequence, a word-loss that changes Lola's behavior, or a specific choice about how she will resume the act inside. Let the last movement do new dramatic work rather than restating the opening thesis.

evidence.excerpt: 2308: Lola sat on her bed after breakfast and tried the word again. Aluminum. There it was. Back in place, as if it had never left, and she couldn't tell — this was the thing, this was the thing that woke her up some nights with her heart fast under her nightgown — she couldn't tell if she'd lost it because she'd been performing confusion for fourteen months and the act had gotten into her, worn a groove, or because she was seventy-nine years old with Parkinson's and sometimes seventy-nine-year-old women with Parkinson's forgot words. Both were true. That was the problem. Both were true and she couldn't tell which one was happening at any given moment, and there was no test for it, no blood draw or brain scan that would come back and say this forgetting is real and this one is theater and this one, Dolores, this one we're not sure about.

2310: She held her left hand up in front of her face. The tremor was there, as it always was — a fine, persistent shaking, worse in the mornings before her carbidopa-levodopa kicked in, worse when she was tired, worse when she was afraid. Right now it was moderate. A ripple, not a quake. She watched it and felt nothing useful. You couldn't argue with a tremor. You couldn't ask it to explain itself. It was just there, the way weather was there, and the medication helped and didn't help, and some mornings she could button her blouse on the first try and some mornings the buttons won and she asked Tammy for help with the same flat acceptance she'd learned to apply to everything her body did without her permission.

2318: But fourteen months of playing confused had done something she hadn't anticipated. The confusion had weight. It pressed down on her actual mind the way water pressed on a sponge — slowly, invisibly, until the sponge was heavy and couldn't remember being dry. She forgot words now. Not performing it, not choosing to stumble for the benefit of whoever was watching, but actually reaching for a word and finding the shelf bare. Aluminum. Thermometer, last week — she'd been trying to tell Carmen her room was cold and she'd said "the thing, the thing that measures" and Carmen had looked at her with that careful nurse's attention and Lola had smiled through it, had made it part of the act, but alone afterward she'd sat on this bed and said thermometer six times fast with her hands balled in her lap. Carnation, the week before that, and that one had frightened her, because Tomás used to bring her carnations, cheap ones from the bodega on Bergenline Avenue, and if she was losing the word for the flower her husband brought her then she was losing a piece of him, and she had so few pieces left.

2326: She sat on the bed and let herself think about him, the way she did most mornings. Not a prayer exactly. A visit. She talked to Tomás in her head — not as delusion, nothing like that, but as habit, the way you talked to someone you'd shared a bed with for forty-three years. The conversation was one-sided and it always had been, because even alive Tomás hadn't been a big talker, and dead he was even less so.

2328: He'd died of pancreatic cancer ten years ago, and he had not gone peacefully. That was the thing about the euphemisms people reached for — passed away, went gently, is in a better place — they were engineering projects, little bridges built over something people didn't want to look down at. What was underneath: Tomás in a hospice bed in their apartment in Union City, a bed the insurance company had delivered with more efficiency than they'd ever shown for his actual treatment, and three months of dying that he'd spent furious. Not sad. Not resigned. Not at-peace-with-God. Furious. He'd cursed the nurses who came to change his IV. He'd cursed the hospice chaplain, a kind young woman named Sister Beth who'd tried to talk to him about acceptance, and Tomás had told her to take her acceptance and get out of his house. He meant it. He was dying at sixty-eight and he had poured concrete and raised two children and paid his taxes and fixed the sink when it leaked and gone to Mass when Lola asked him to and now his pancreas was eating itself alive and no one could tell him why.

2342: Her daughter Miriam sent cards from Orlando. Birthday, Christmas, Mother's Day, and random Hallmark cards with flowers on the front and messages like "Thinking of you today!" The exclamation point always felt wrong to Lola, something pushed when it should be quiet. If you were thinking of someone, you could come. But Miriam had two kids and a husband who traveled for work and a mortgage she'd mentioned twice with the specific worry that meant it was too much, and Orlando was far, and Lola didn't blame her. She didn't blame either of them. They were living the lives America demanded of children — spread out, working, building families in whatever city had jobs — and the alternative was guilt, which helped no one and which Lola had seen do terrible things to other families in this building. The daughter in Hackensack who visited every day and cried every time, until the resident — a man named Phil who had Lewy body dementia and didn't know anyone's name — started getting agitated whenever she showed up. The son who came once a month from Pennsylvania and spent the entire visit staring at his phone. Guilt made people stupid. Lola preferred the cards.

2344: She touched the crucifix at her throat. The gold was warm from her skin. She didn't pray — she hadn't prayed since Tomás died, because she had some things to say to God and none of them were requests — but the crucifix was her mother's, and touching it was a way of touching her mother, dead longer than Tomás and missed in a way that lived in Lola's hands, in the specific shape her fingers made when they closed around the small gold cross.

2376: It was not fine and not whatever and they both knew it. Lola could hear the specific frequency of Tammy's not-fine, the way her consonants sharpened and the space between words stretched, as if she were testing each one's ability to hold her weight before she set it down. Tammy's divorce had surfaced over these courtyard afternoons in pieces, not chronologically, not as a story she was telling, but the way someone emptied a bag onto a table: a detail here, a fact there, scattered and unsorted. Greg had left in March. He'd been seeing someone before that — not Brandy, someone else, a woman from his gym whose name Tammy had never said out loud, which told Lola more than the name would have. Papers were final in July. Tammy had moved into the studio in Perth Amboy with her cat, who'd immediately peed on the mattress. She'd replaced the mattress, not the cat. That detail had been delivered with the particular deadpan that meant Tammy was choosing between laughing and something worse.

2400: Lola felt the guilt arrive the way it always did when Tammy told her something real — not as a pang or a wave but as a weight, a physical addition to what she was already carrying, settling in next to the grief and the tremor and the fear about the words she kept losing. She was deceiving this woman. Every afternoon, every cup of coffee, every time Tammy sat on this bench and said things that mattered to her, Lola was receiving them through a screen of performed confusion, and Tammy didn't know. Tammy brought her coffee and brushed her hair and told her about Greg and the apartment and the bad lighting and the cat, and Lola took all of it and gave back a character. A warm character. A character who cared, because Lola did care, that was the terrible part — the caring was real and the person doing the caring was a fraud, and those two facts existed in the same body and she couldn't separate them any more than she could separate the performance tremor from the Parkinson's tremor.

2446: Her left hand trembled against her thigh. She put her right hand over it, covering it, the way you'd hold a small animal that was shaking — not to stop the shaking but to let it know the shaking had been noticed.

2448: The word came back to her. Aluminum. It had been there all along, she supposed. It was the not-knowing that was the thing. Not the forgetting — everybody forgot, that was just the tax on being alive long enough — but the not-knowing which kind of forgetting it was. Whether the word had gone because she'd spent fourteen months teaching her brain to lose things on purpose, or because her brain was losing things on its own now, because seventy-nine years and Parkinson's and grief and a daily performance of incapacity had, together, begun to do what each one alone might not have managed.

findings.problem: Full-book pattern (all 18 chapters). This chapter has the HIGHEST density of auto-commentary constructions in the novel — at least 13 instances across 154 lines (~1 per 12 lines). This is particularly damaging because Chapter 12 is Lola's POV, which the style bible specifies should feel 'winding and emotionally layered, with embedded memories.' Instead, the narration sounds like the same analytical voice explaining Lola to the reader. Specific instances: 'this was the thing, this was the thing' (line 2308 — doubled narrator pivot); 'the way weather was there' (line 2310); 'the way water pressed on a sponge' (line 2318); 'the way she did most mornings' (line 2326); 'That was the thing about the euphemisms' (line 2328 — second narrator pivot in 20 lines); 'the specific worry that meant it was too much' (line 2342); 'the specific shape her fingers made' (line 2344); 'the specific frequency of Tammy's not-fine, the way her consonants sharpened' (line 2376 — 'the specific' + 'the way' stacked); 'the way it always did... not as a pang or a wave but as a weight' (line 2400 — 'the way' + 'not X but Y' stacked); 'the way you'd hold a small animal' (line 2446); 'the not-knowing that was the thing' (line 2448 — third narrator pivot). The underlying material in this chapter is among the novel's best — the Tomás death scene, the Tammy courtyard conversation, the aluminum anxiety. The formulas drone over it.
findings.rewrite_direction: REPLACE — retain 0 instances; chapters 1 and 15 are the retention candidates for the full novel. This chapter needs the most aggressive formula reduction. Target lines: (1) Lines 2308 and 2448: cut both 'this/that was the thing' pivots. The aluminum anxiety and the not-knowing are already dramatized — the chapter opens and closes with Lola testing the word. The narrator doesn't need to announce the theme. (2) Line 2318: cut 'the way water pressed on a sponge.' Lola's narration should think in stories and concrete memories (Tomás, carnations, bodega on Bergenline Avenue), not in explanatory similes. Replace the simile with a memory or an image from Lola's life. (3) Line 2326: cut 'the way she did most mornings' and 'the way you talked to someone' — let Lola's habit of talking to Tomás be shown through the act itself. (4) Line 2328: 'That was the thing about the euphemisms' — cut the announcement and lead directly with the euphemisms themselves; Lola would tell it as a story, not a thesis. (5) Lines 2342, 2344: cut both 'the specific' qualifiers. (6) Line 2376: cut 'the specific frequency of Tammy's not-fine' — replace with Lola noticing something concrete about Tammy (her body, her hands, the Styrofoam cup). (7) Line 2400: cut both 'the way it always did' and 'not as a pang or a wave but as a weight' — replace the triple-option simile with one concrete physical sensation. (8) Line 2446: cut 'the way you'd hold a small animal.' Lola puts her right hand over her left. That's enough. General: Lola's POV should arrive through stories, through sensory memories (Tomás's hands, the bodega, the carnations), through the braided structure the style bible describes — not through the same analytical glossing every other chapter uses.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This is a dark comedy about elderly agency, institutional failure, and moral compromise in a setting most fiction treats as a punchline or a tragedy but never both. The risks the novel must take: render the comedy of octogenarians as pharmaceutical distributors without condescending to them or turning them into lovable rogues; make Carmen's escalating complicity fully legible without moralizing about it; show the bodily realities of aging — incontinence, tremor, cognitive decline, falls, the smell of a body that doesn't leave a building — with unsentimental specificity; render the economic desperation of both healthcare workers and uninsured buyers without turning either into social-issue abstractions; give the moral ambiguity of the ring genuine weight by refusing to resolve it into either 'they were wrong' or 'they were heroes.' The novel must trust readers to sit with complexity. When in doubt between too raw and too safe, choose raw. A nursing home that smells like potpourri is a nursing home written by someone who's never been in one.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism for bodily functions, aging, economic shame, institutional dysfunction, or the realities of nursing home care is a craft failure. The novel's comedy and humanity depend on rendering what polite fiction glosses over: what it smells like to clean someone who can't clean themselves, what it costs to count coins for a bus fare, what oxycodone sells for per milligram on the secondary market, what a concussion looks like on an 88-year-old skull, what it sounds like when someone with Parkinson's tries to hold a coffee cup steady, what Burt's dementia actually looks like up close. If the prose flinches from these specifics, the novel loses its authority.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the default for all dialogue. Uncontracted forms appear only for emphasis, character-specific formality (Agnes's low contraction level), Carmen under extreme stress reverting to formal speech, or deliberate register choices. In a contemporary New Jersey nursing home, stiff diction is the anomaly, not the norm.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate high-intensity scenes with reflective interiority. Vary scene count: single-scene chapters for maximum compression, three-scene chapters for range. Comic chapters (3, 9) should feel structurally different from quiet character studies (7, 8, 12, 15), which should feel different from crisis chapters (11, 16, 17). Follow a confrontation with a reflection, an escalation with a domestic scene, a comic set piece with an elegiac one. If two consecutive chapters share the same rhythm, one needs redesigning.
prose_style_profile.diction: Grounded and specific. Drug names (oxycodone, alprazolam, hydroxychloroquine), nursing procedures (med pass, pill count, charting, fall assessment), body realities (incontinence, tremor, laceration, scalp bleed). No purple prose, no literary performance. The style should feel like someone who works with their hands is telling you the truth about what she saw.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, varying by POV character. Carmen chapters are warm and sensory, anchored in the body and the immediate environment. Harry chapters are precise and calculating, with attention to numbers and angles. Agnes chapters are clinical and compressed. Lola chapters are winding and emotionally layered, with embedded memories. Earl chapters are spare and weighted, with silence doing as much work as language. Marcus chapters are kinetic, with physical movement and code-switching. Tammy chapters are flat and observational, her trained eye cataloguing what doesn't add up.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied by chapter and POV. Carmen chapters favor medium sentences with occasional long runs when she's anxious. Harry chapters are short and clipped. Comic set pieces (chapters 3 and 9) move between fast inventory and slow domestic beats. Quiet chapters (7, 12, 15) slow down and let space onto the page. No chapter should feel rhythmically identical to the one before it.
